key_themes: Shareholder disappointment over valuation and excess capital
Irish government exit from post-crisis banking stake
Extended timeline to completion (Q4 2026 or Q1 2027)
Rare market reaction with buyer (BAWAG) shares rising while PTSB falls
summary: Permanent TSB (PTSB) is being acquired by Austrian lender BAWAG in a recommended €1.62 billion cash deal at €2.97 per share, allowing the Irish government to fully exit its 57.5% crisis-era stake for approximately €931 million.[[1]](https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/austrias-bawag-buy-irish-lender-permanent-tsb-19-billion-2026-04-14/)[[2]](https://www.reuters.com/company/permanent-tsb-group-holdings-plc/) While this provides certainty and a premium to levels when the sale process launched, investor and analyst reaction has been largely negative, with the price widely viewed as disappointing relative to standalone potential, excess capital (CET1 well above target, implying ~€450m surplus in some analyses), and prior analyst targets around €3.10.[[3]](https://croninj.substack.com/p/ptsb-sale-perspectives-uk-banks-m)[[4]](https://www.investing.com/equities/permanent-tsb) PTSB shares fell on announcement and continue trading below the offer (near €2.87–2.90), reflecting the long wait to closing, minor approval risks (75% shareholder support required), and time value. BAWAG shares rose sharply, with the deal labeled accretive and even “deal of the century” by some.[[5]](https://www.irishtimes.com/business/financial-services/2026/04/14/austrian-banking-group-bawag-wins-race-to-buy-ptsb-for-almost-162bn/)
sources: 10+ news articles (Reuters, Irish Times, Business Post, Yahoo Finance, company releases), 15+ relevant X posts from April 2026 discussing deal reactions
